segments.s040-g1: برآمد is a past-tense narrative verb meaning 'arose/appeared'. بر + ین here form the demonstrative compound برین (literally 'on/this'), functioning poetically to mark the following noun; روزگار is the noun 'time/age' and دراز is the adjective 'long' placed after it (typical of older Persian word order). The phrase literally reads 'arose this long age' = 'a long age arose'.
segments.s040-g2: زمانه is 'the time/age' (subject); به دل در is a prepositional phrase 'in the heart' (به + دل, with در reinforcing interiority); همی is an archaic continuative/emphatic particle that here accompanies داشت to indicate a held/continuing state; داشت is past 'had/held'; راز is the direct object 'secret'. Together: 'the times held a secret in the heart.'
segments.s040-g3: فریدون is the proper name. فرزانه شد is the verb phrase 'became wise' (فرزانه = 'wise', شد = 'became'); سالخورد is an adjectival/participial attribute meaning 'aged' or 'grown old' placed after the verb for poetic effect. The sequence means 'Fereydun became wise and aged.'
segments.s040-g4: به باغ بهار اندر is a prepositional group 'into the garden of spring' (اندر = archaic 'in/within'). آورد is the verb 'brought/caused to go' used here of movement into the place, and گرد is a verbal/adverbial particle (poetic) meaning 'to go/turn about' (i.e. indicating movement). Together they describe his moving into the spring garden: 'he went/entered into the spring garden.'
segments.s040-g5: برین گونه is a fixed phrase 'in this manner/thus' (بر + این in archaic spelling + گونه 'manner'). گردد is a present/subjunctive or narrative verb 'becomes/turns' used here in a generalizing statement, and سراسر سخن ('all speech' or 'the whole discourse') is the affected domain. Literally: 'In this way the whole speech turns.'
segments.s040-g6: شود is another verb 'becomes' (here stating the resultant state). سست نیرو = 'weak (is) strength' (adjective before noun for emphasis). چو introduces the temporal conditional 'when/once', گردد that follows is 'becomes', and کهن is 'old.' The clause reads: 'strength becomes weak when it becomes old.' Note the doubled verbs (گردد/شود) are a poetic way to state general truth.
segments.s040-g7: چو آمد is the temporal clause 'when/when it came' (چو = 'when'). به + کاراندرون = 'into the inner affairs/inner court' (کاراندرون is an older compound meaning domestic or inner matters). تیرگی is the noun 'darkness/gloom' and functions as the subject that 'came' into that inner sphere: 'when gloom entered the inner affairs.'
segments.s040-g8: گرفتند is a past-tense verb 'they were seized/they took (upon themselves)' (here effectively passive: 'were seized'). پرمایگان is the plural subject 'the nobles/the great ones', and خیرگی is the result 'astonishment/stupefaction.' Word order is poetic (verb before subject): 'the nobles were seized by astonishment.'
segments.s040-g57: This is a locative/idiomatic phrase: 'به جای' (= 'in the place of' / 'to be placed into') governs the nouns that follow. 'جای زبونی' = 'the place/state of abasement' and 'جای فریب' = 'the place/state of deception' are coordinated by 'و'. The whole phrase is the complement/topic that the following clause will deny as appropriate for the hero.
segments.s040-g58: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal meaning 'it should not / must not (be the case)'; it introduces a subordinate clause with 'که' ('that'). 'یابد' is a present/subjunctive form of 'یافتن' meaning 'find/come into' (here expressing the outcome one should not reach). 'دلاور شکیب' is a noun phrase 'the brave, patient one' (شکیب an archaic adjective 'patient'), the logical subject/experiencer of 'یابد'.
segments.s040-g59: 'نشاید' is an impersonal predicate meaning 'it is not fitting' or 'one must not'; it takes the following noun phrase as what should not occur. 'درنگ' = 'delay/pausing' is the prohibited action, 'اندرین کار' = 'in this matter/affair' locates it, and 'هیچ' intensifies the negation ('at all' / 'no').
segments.s040-g60: Rhetorical question/statement: 'کجا آید' ('where comes' / 'where is it that ... comes') + 'آسایش' ('comfort, ease') asks where comfort is found; 'اندر بسیچ' = 'in great amount / in abundance' modifies 'آسایش'. Together: 'where does comfort come in abundance?' (implying: nowhere).
segments.s040-g61: 'فرستاده' is the subject 'the messenger / envoy.' 'چون' here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when / as.' 'پاسخ آورد' is a past-tense verb phrase 'brought the reply' (آورد = 'brought'). The adverb 'باز' after the verb emphasizes return/backness ('brought back' / 'returned with').
segments.s040-g62: 'برهنه شد' is a past passive/perfect construction 'was made bare / became uncovered.' 'آن روی' = 'that face' (demonstrative + noun) and 'پوشیده‌راز' is a compound adjective 'secretly covered / veiled (in secrecy)' modifying 'روی'; together the phrase means the previously hidden/veiled face was revealed.
segments.s040-g63: A compound past narrative: 'برفت' (archaic/past 'he went / departed') introduces the action. 'این برادر' names one brother ('this brother') and 'ز روم' specifies his origin ('from Rum'). The second member 'آن ز چین' parallels the first: 'that (one) from China.' The clause lists two brothers and their respective departures.
segments.s040-g64: 'به زهر اندر آمیخته انگبین' describes the state of the honey/nectar: 'انگبین' = 'honey/nectar' is 'آمیخته' (mixed/blended) 'اندر' (in/with) 'زهر' (poison). The preposition 'به' introduces the mixture/association ('with / into'); the phrase means the nectar had been mingled with poison.
segments.s040-g17: فرستاد is a perfective past verb 'sent' with an omitted subject (he/they). نزد + noun marks the destination/presence ('to / in the presence of'), so نزد برادر پیام is a prepositional phrase giving the addressee; پیام is the content/object 'a message'. Word order is verb → prepositional phrase + object, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s040-g18: که introduces a reported clause ('that') giving the message's content. جاوید is an adjective 'everlasting/eternal' placed before the imperative verb زی ('live' — here the archaic/poetic imperative of زیستن). خرم and شادکام are coordinate adjectives linked by و describing the wished state. Together this is a direct quoted wish: 'Live forever, joyful and fortunate.'
segments.s040-g19: بدان‌ای is the imperative 'know!' (بدان) fused with the vocative particle ای (addressing someone): 'Know, O ...'. شهنشاه is the vocative 'king of kings' or 'emperor', followed by the compounds ترکان و چین listing realms ('the Turks and China') — here naming the addressee's domains.
segments.s040-g20: گسسته is a past participle/adjectival form 'rent/broken' modifying دل 'heart' (گسسته دل = 'a heart that is torn'). روشن functions as a descriptive adjective 'clear/bright' that is linked to the following phrase introduced by از; here از means 'because of / from' and the phrase از به گزین (به + گزین) refers to 'in/through choosing (the good)' (گزین = 'to choose' used nominally), so the segment describes a heart torn yet clear/steadfast regarding its choice.
segments.s040-g21: ز (poetic form of از) marks source or cause ('from / because of'). نیکی is 'goodness / good deed'. زیان کرده is a compound where زیان ('loss, harm') + کرده (preterite/participle 'has done') yields 'has suffered harm' or 'has been harmed'. گویی here functions as a parenthetical particle meaning 'one would say' / 'it seems', and پسند is a noun 'liking / preference' — so the clause says 'because of goodness he has suffered loss; one would say (he has) a preference ...'.
segments.s040-g22: منش is the noun 'character / disposition' followed by two adjectives joined by و: پست 'low, base' and بالا 'high/lofty' — here the contrast suggests 'prefers baseness despite being lofty'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', linking سرو بلند 'a tall (lofty) cypress' as the image for physical height or appearance. Adjectives follow their nouns in this line and the simile modifies the previous description.
segments.s040-g23: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. بشنو is the imperative 'listen/hear'. ازمن 'from me' gives the source of the tale, and یکی داستان 'one tale / a certain story' is the direct object — typical Persian order here places the imperative verb first, followed by source and object.
segments.s040-g24: کزین = 'from/of this' (poetic), گونه = 'kind / manner', together 'of this sort'. نشنیدی is the simple past (2nd sg) with negative sense 'you have not heard (of)'; از باستان 'from antiquity / since ancient times' indicates that such a tale is unheard of from old times. The clause therefore says 'a story of this kind you have not heard from ancient times.'
segments.s040-g65: رسیدند is a past plural verb 'they arrived/approached'. پس here is a simple connective 'then'. یک به دیگر is a fixed phrase 'one to another' (literal order: one to another), with به marking the target; فراز is an adverbial/poetic modifier placed after the phrase meaning 'aloud/on high'. The whole segment says that they approached one another and did so openly/aloft.
segments.s040-g66: سخن is the noun 'speech/words' (direct topic). راندند is past plural 'they drove/uttered' used here in a poetic sense 'they uttered/spoke'. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly', و is the coordinating 'and', and راز is the noun 'secret'. Together this says they delivered words both openly and (about) secrets.
segments.s040-g67: گزیدند is past plural 'they chose/selected'. پس is 'then'. موبدی is the object 'a mobad' (a Zoroastrian priest), and تیزویر is an epithet/name attached to موبدی; تیزویر is an uncommon/compound epithet and is treated here as a name/descriptor following the noun.
segments.s040-g68: This is a string of epithets describing the chosen mobad. سخن is 'word/speech' forming the compound idea 'sokhan-gū(ī)' (one who speaks). گوی here functions as a deverbal/nominative element meaning 'speaker' rather than the literal senses given, so no exact sense matches. و links the next epithet بینادل (a rare/uncertain word, left untranslated as an epithet), و links the final epithet یادگیر 'one who remembers/learns'. Adjectival/epithet elements follow the noun in this poetic word-order.
segments.s040-g69: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/for' indicating source or beneficiary; بیگانه is the noun 'stranger/foreigner' and serves as the source/beneficiary of the action. پردخته کردند is a past plural verbal phrase 'they prepared/adorned' (پردخته used adjectivally/participial 'well‑worked/arranged'), and جای 'place' is the direct object placed after the verb for poetic order.
segments.s040-g70: سگالش is a rare/uncertain term here (treated as the thing they take or accept), and گرفتند is past plural 'they took/accepted'. هر گونه is the quantifying phrase 'every kind of' (هر = every, گونه = kind/type) modifying رای 'counsel/opinion'. The clause therefore says they accepted every sort of counsel (the rare term سگالش being the item taken).
segments.s040-g71: سخن سلم is a possessive/genitive construction 'the speech of Salm' (Salm is a proper name). پیوند کرد is past 'he joined/linked' but in this idiom means 'he began/commenced (the speech)'; از نخست is an adverbial 'from the first/at the outset'. Note that سلم is a proper name here (not the dictionary senses), so the listed senses do not apply.
segments.s040-g72: ز is the preposition 'out of/because of'. شرم پدر is a genitive phrase 'shame before his father' (i.e., shame/respect toward the father). دیدگان را is the definite object 'the eyes' with the accusative marker را, and بشست is the past 3rd‑singular 'he washed/wiped'. The clause literally says he washed his eyes because of shame (i.e., wiped away his tears from shame/respect for his father).
segments.s040-g9: Poetic word order: the verb جنبید (stirred/moved) comes first and governs the rest of the clause. دل ز جای (“heart from its place”) is the core predication, with را marking سلم as a definite/affected noun associated by possession or focus; the phrase as a whole means “Salm’s heart was moved from its place” (i.e., his heart was shaken).
segments.s040-g10: دگرگونه‌تر is a comparative/adjective ‘more different’ (here 'different'), followed by شد (past ‘became’), so the construction means ‘he became different.’ به before آیین marks the domain (‘in/with regard to custom/practice’), and رای (‘counsel/plan’) is coordinated by و, so together: ‘became different in manner and counsel.’
segments.s040-g11: دلش is the possessed noun ‘his heart’ (دل + enclitic ش), گشت is past ‘became/turned,’ and غرقه is an adjective ‘immersed/drenched.’ به + آزاندرون marks the locus or object of immersion (a proper name/place); the clause means ‘his heart became immersed in Āzāndarun.’
segments.s040-g12: The clause begins with به اندیشه (‘into/in thought’) indicating manner; بنشست is the past verb ‘sat down’ (here intransitive, meaning ‘took a seat/settled’ mentally), and با رهنمون (‘with a guide/guidance’) attaches as an accompanying phrase. Overall: ‘he sat in thought with a guide.’
segments.s040-g13: نبودش is a contracted past construction meaning ‘it was not’ (negated copula applied to the following predicate); پسندیده is a predicate adjective ‘pleasing/approved,’ and بخش پدر is a genitive construction ‘the father's gift/portion.’ The whole clause reads ‘the father's gift was not pleasing/approved.’
segments.s040-g14: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ‘that/which’; داد is past ‘gave,’ او is the subject ‘he,’ به marks the indirect object, کهتر پسر is ‘the lesser/younger son’ (کهتر = inferior/younger), and تخت زر ‘golden throne’ is the direct object (noun + modifier). The clause specifies the content of the father’s displeasing gift: ‘that he gave the golden throne to the younger son.’
segments.s040-g15: This is a parallel pair of predicative phrases. به دل پر ز کین شد: به + دل ‘in/with respect to the heart,’ پر ز کین ‘full of hatred/vengeance,’ and شد ‘became’ — ‘his heart became full of hatred.’ The second phrase به رخ پر ز چین (with رخ ‘face’ and چین ‘wrinkle’) is parallel and has an implied شد (elliptical), giving ‘his face full of wrinkles.’
segments.s040-g16: Two verbs of sending appear: فرسته (a ‘messenger/one sent’) followed by فرستاد (past ‘sent’), a common epic compression where a noun and verb pair to mean ‘sent a messenger.’ زی before شاه چین is a classical preposition meaning ‘to/unto’ (here introducing the recipient), so the clause reads ‘he sent an envoy to the King of China.’
segments.s040-g49: درختیست is the poetic contraction of درخت است (‘it is a tree’), with the copula attached to the noun. این خود نشانده بدست is a noun phrase and participial clause: این خود (‘this very thing’) + نشانده (past participle ‘placed/ set’) + بدست (‘by the hand/of’), so together ‘this very thing set by the hand’. Word order is noun-copula then the participial phrase describing who set it.
segments.s040-g50: کجا introduces a relative/locative clause ‘where…’. آب او خون reads literally ‘its water [is] blood’ (آب = sap/water, او = its/he/it, خون = blood); the copula is omitted as normal in Persian. و links the two predicates. برگش would normally be ‘its leaves’ here (برگ + -ش), but the provided senses list lacks that meaning, and کبست is an archaic/obscure lexeme used predicatively for the leaves.
segments.s040-g51: ترا is the older/object form ‘تو را’ (you-OBJ) written as suffix plus را; با من ‘with me’ and اکنون ‘now’ are adverbial/agent phrases. بدین گفت‌گوی = به + این + گفت‌گوی ‘by/with this conversation’ (instrumental), so the whole phrase means ‘you, with me, now by this conversation…’. Note archaic segmentation where بدین is written with ب separate here.
segments.s040-g52: بباید is the modal ‘must/ought’ (old/poetic ordering allows modal before the personal verb). بروی is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form ‘you go/you should go’. اندر آورد روی is a light verbal phrase: اندر ‘into/ before’ + آورد ‘bring’ + روی ‘face’ = ‘bring (it) before (the face)’, i.e. ‘present it to him’. The object of آورد is left implicit but understood from context.
segments.s040-g53: رای زدن and نگاه کردن are noun+light-verb constructions: رای زدن ‘to express/put forward an opinion/counsel’ and نگاه کردن ‘to look/to cast a glance’. هشیار postmodifies رای (‘a prudent/sensible counsel’). The coordinating و links the two actions. کردن here functions as the light/auxiliary verb in نگاه کردن.
segments.s040-g54: هیونی (an archaic/poetic form of هیوان) is a beast/monstrous creature and is the object of the verb فگندن (‘to throw’). به نزدیک شاه gives the direction/goal ‘toward/close to the king’. Note the older spelling فگندن (modern فکندن/انداختن).
segments.s040-g55: زبان‌آوری is a noun meaning ‘speech/eloquence’ or by extension ‘one who speaks’; چرب (‘oily’) here means ‘flattering’; گوی can mean ‘speaker’/‘one who utters’ or ‘saying’ — together the phrase describes a flattering, smooth-tongued speaker. از میان is the prepositional ‘from among’. Adjectives and modifiers are ordered poetically, with چرب qualifying the speaking.
segments.s040-g56: فرستاد باید is an older word order for باید فرستاد (‘one must send’). به شاه جهان is the dative phrase ‘to the king of the world’ (شاه جهان = an honorific title for the king). The construction expresses necessity that such a flattering speaker be dispatched to the king.
segments.s040-g81: چه سازی is an exclamatory colloquial/poetic construction equivalent to 'what are you doing' (literally 'what making'); سازی is a verbal noun/derived present form (poetic omission of the helper mi-). درنگ is the object 'lingering/delay'. اندرین جای تنگ is a prepositional/attributive phrase 'in this narrow place' (اندرین = 'in this', جای = 'place', تنگ = 'narrow').
segments.s040-g82: که introduces a reason clause here ('since/for/that'). شد is the simple past 'became'. تنگ is predicate adjective 'narrow/cramped'. بر تو marks the beneficiary/affected person 'for/upon you'. سرای درنگ is a nominal phrase 'the abode/house of delay' (word order is poetic: verb precedes or follows phrases; literally 'became narrow upon you the abode of delay').
segments.s040-g83: This is a compact past statement: داد is the past 'gave'. یزدان پاک 'the pure God' is the agent. جهان is the thing given ('the world'). مرترا appears as the indirect object or vocative (form is irregular/uncertain in the provided lexicon), i.e. 'to you' or a proper name; its exact sense is ambiguous in the given entries.
segments.s040-g84: ز ... تا introduces a range: ز = 'from', تا = 'as far as / to'. تابنده is a participial adjective 'shining/radiant' modifying خورشید 'sun'. تیره خاک is a noun phrase 'dark earth/dust' (تیره = 'dark', خاک = 'earth'). Together they specify the extent of what was given.
segments.s040-g85: همه qualifies the objects (all/everything). بآرزو is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'with longing/with desire' (ب = preposition + آرزو). ساختی is the 2nd-person past 'you built/made/fashioned'. رسم و راه are the direct objects 'customs and ways.' Word order places the adverbial and objects after the verb in poetic usage.
segments.s040-g86: نکردی is the negated past form 'you did not (do/observe)'. به فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'to/by the command of God' (فرمان یزدان = 'God's command'). نگاه here is a noun meaning 'regard/heed' (i.e. 'pay heed to'); the phrase means 'you did not heed God's command.'
segments.s040-g87: نجستی is a past form from جست (here 'you sought/sought for'); in context it means 'you sought/aimed at'. به جز is the fixed expression 'except for / other than'. کژی و کاستی are the nouns 'crookedness/wrongness and deficiency/shortcoming' as the only things sought.
segments.s040-g88: نکردی again is the negative past 'you did not do'. به here introduces the manner/means 'by/through'. بخشش is used as 'giving/grant' (sense: donation/giving) and درون راستی forms a genitive or adjectival phrase 'inward truthfulness' or 'inner righteousness'—so the clause says 'you did not, by giving, show/keep inward truthfulness.'
segments.s040-g25: This clause states the subject and its description: 'سه فرزند بودیم' is literal S‑V: numeral + noun + past‑tense copula 'بودیم' (we were). 'زیبای تخت' is a poetic epithet where 'زیبای' modifies 'تخت' ('beauty of the throne' / 'beautiful of the throne') with the ezāfe implied; the adjective precedes the head noun in the line for metrical reasons.
segments.s040-g26: 'یکی' = 'one (of us)'; 'کهتر از ما' is a comparative clause formed with 'کهتر' + 'از' + pronoun meaning 'lesser/younger than us'; 'برآمد' is a past intransitive verb 'arose/appeared'; 'به بخت' is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'by/through fortune' (here indicating cause or agency).
segments.s040-g27: 'اگر' introduces a conditional/concessive construction. 'مهترم' is the adjective 'مهتر' ('elder/senior') used predicatively with the first‑person enclitic '-م' giving 'I am the elder'; the following 'من' is a resumptive/emphatic pronoun. 'به سال و خرد' means 'in respect of age and wisdom' (به + noun = 'in/regarding').
segments.s040-g28: 'زمانه' (the times/age) is the subject; 'به مهر من' is a prepositional phrase 'to/into my favour' where 'مهر' = 'favour/affection'; 'اندر خورد' is a compound classical/periphrastic verbal phrase meaning 'fell into/was won' — 'اندر' (in) precedes the simple past 'خورد' (befell/struck) to indicate the resultant state.
segments.s040-g29: 'گذشته ز من' uses 'گذشته' as a past/participle meaning 'that has gone/passed from me' with the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from'; the list 'تاج و تخت و کلاه' enumerates the items lost (crown, throne, cap). The participial phrase precedes the items for emphasis.
segments.s040-g30: 'نزیبد' (from 'زیبیدن') is an impersonal/experiential verb meaning 'it befits/it becomes (not)'; here it has the sense 'does not befit' or 'would not suit.' 'مگر' = 'except'; 'بر تو‌ای پادشاه' = 'upon you, O king' (vocative 'تو-ای' addresses the king directly). The implied subject is the things listed earlier.
segments.s040-g31: 'سزدگر' is a classical predicative adjective meaning 'fitting/appropriate'; when combined with the verb 'بمانیم' (1st‑person plural present/subjunctive 'let us remain' or 'we should remain') it yields an optative sense: 'it would be fitting that we both remain...'. 'هر دو' = 'both'; 'دژم' = 'sullen/grim' describes their mood.
segments.s040-g32: 'کزین سان' = 'from/through this way' (literal 'from this such'); 'پدر کرد' is simple past S‑V: 'the father did/made'; 'بر ما ستم' is a prepositional object 'oppression/ wrong upon us' (بر = upon/against, ما = us, ستم = oppression). Word order is typical classical Persian: circumstantial phrase + agent + verb + prepositional object.
segments.s040-g105: "و گرنه" is a conditional/contrastive phrase meaning "and otherwise / or else" linking this clause to what precedes. "سواران ترکان و چین" is a single noun phrase: "سواران" (horsemen/riders) is the head noun, and "ترکان و چین" (the Turks and Chin/China) are the possessor/identifying groups; poetic word order omits an explicit ezāfe but the relation is genitival.
segments.s040-g106: "هم" is an adverb meaning "also/too," marking that the following group is included as well. "از روم گردان" is a prepositional noun phrase "troops/troop(s) from Rum (Byzantium)" with "گردان" used as "troop." "جوینده کین" is a participial/adjectival phrase: "جوینده" (seeking) modifies the implied subject (the troop), and "کین" is its object "vengeance." The clause is elliptical (no finite main verb) and reads as a conditional future/optative sense.
segments.s040-g107: "فراز آورم" is a verbal phrase where "آورم" is first-person present/future "I bring/I will bring" and "فراز" is an adverbial "on high/aloft" placed before the verb for emphasis. "لشگر گرزدار" is a noun phrase "an army (لشگر) of mace-bearers," with "گرزدار" postpositive as an adjectival qualifier.
segments.s040-g108: "از ایران و ایرج" is a prepositional source phrase "from Iran and Irāj" (ایرج = personal name). "برآرم" is the same first-person verb with the intensifying prefix "بر-" meaning "I will raise/bring about." "دمار" is the direct object "ruin/devastation." The verb-object order follows standard Persian (verb then object), and the whole clause continues the speaker's threat.
segments.s040-g109: "چو بشنید" is a temporal clause "when he heard," where "چو" = "when" and "بشنید" is the past tense 3rd-person singular of "شنیدن." "موبد پیام درشت" gives the subject and its object: "موبد" (the mobed/priest) and "پیام درشت" (a coarse/harsh message), with "درشت" postpositive modifying "پیام."
segments.s040-g110: "زمین را ببوسید" is a past tense clause "he kissed the ground," where "را" marks the definite object and "ببوسید" is the (poetically prefixed) past form of "بوسیدن." "و بنمود پشت" continues with "and he showed/turned (his) back," where "بنمود" is past "he showed/turned" and "پشت" is the noun "back," the action of turning away.
segments.s040-g111: "بر آنسان" means "in that manner/thus." "به زین اندر آورد پای" is a light-verb construction meaning "he put/placed his foot into the saddle": "به زین" = "to/on the saddle," "اندر آورد" = "brought in/put in," and "پای" = "(his) foot." The verb "آورد" is past (he brought/put).
segments.s040-g112: "که" introduces the result/purpose clause "so that/that." "از باد آتش" is a poetic genitive phrase "from a wind of fire" (i.e., as if by a fiery gust). "بجنبد" is a present/subjunctive-form verb meaning "may move / might be moved" (classical subjunctive after که). "ز جای" means "from (his) place." The clause therefore describes the forceful beginning of movement.
segments.s040-g121: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking source/origin; چندان is a quantifying modifier ('so many' / 'so great') that precedes the adjective گرانمایه ('esteemed, precious'). گرد + دلیر here gives location/association: گرد functions adverbially as 'around' and دلیر is the noun 'the brave/hero' that it relates to — together meaning 'around the hero.' The whole chunk is a prepositional/causal phrase supplying where/from whom the sound comes.
segments.s040-g122: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/clamor' and برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth' (third-person). چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as', connecting the following noun phrase; آوای is 'voice/sound' and شیر is 'lion' — together 'a roar arose like the voice of a lion.' Word order is typical poetic inversion but straightforward: subject (خروشی) + verb (برآمد) + simile clause (چو آوای شیر).
segments.s040-g123: سپهریست is a fused adjectival/predicative form meaning 'heavenly/like the sky.' پنداشت is a past-tense verb 'thought/imagined' (third-person) taking ایوان ('iwan/porch') as its object. به جای is a locative phrase 'as a place' / 'to be a place' — together the clause means 'they imagined the iwan to be celestial / like the heavens.' Note the poetic ordering where the adjective-predicate appears before the verb of thinking.
segments.s040-g124: گران is an attributive adjective 'heavy / mighty' modifying لشگری ('a host/army'). گرد functions as 'around' marking spatial relation; او is the pronoun 'him' (the person around whom they gather). بر + به پای forms a locative expression meaning 'on/upon at the foot (of)' — together: 'a mighty host gathered around him at his feet.' The noun phrase (گران لشگری) is the subject and the following locatives give its position.
segments.s040-g125: برفتند is a past-tense plural verb 'they went/left.' In this poetic line the subjects (descriptive nouns) follow or cluster with the verb: بیدار ('awake/alert') and کارآگهان (plural/collective 'the informed/knowledgeable ones') characterize who moved. The phrase therefore reads 'the alert, informed men went.'
segments.s040-g126: بگفتند is a past plural 'they said/spoke.' با is the preposition 'with' marking the interlocutor(s) or accompaniment; شهریار جهان is a titular noun phrase 'the king (lord) of the world' (شهریار = king, جهان = world) — so 'they spoke with the king of the world.' The clause links as a coordination after the previous verb (they went and they spoke).
segments.s040-g127: که introduces a clause 'that/for' (here giving the reported event). آمد is the past-tense verb 'came.' فرستاده‌ای is the noun-formed participle 'a sent one' i.e. 'a messenger' (here used as the subject/actor). نزد is the preposition 'in the presence of / to' marking the addressee, and شاه is 'king' — together 'that a messenger came to the king.'
segments.s040-g128: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one' introducing the appositive description. پرمنش is an adjective 'high‑minded / well‑bred / dignified' modifying مرد ('man'). با is the preposition 'with' introducing دستگاه ('entourage / court / retinue') — 'a certain dignified man with an entourage.' This appositive phrase further identifies the messenger.
segments.s040-g73: فرستاده را گفت is a past-tense reporting clause: 'he said to the messenger', where را marks the (indirect) object/topic. ره برنورد is the reported verb phrase (literal: 'traverse the road') functioning as the content of what was said; the bare verbal form برنورد is a classical/poetic verbal form meaning 'to go across/traverse'.
segments.s040-g74: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason/consequence. یابد is a present/subjunctive-form of 'to find' used here with an optative/possibility sense ('that ... may/find you'), ترا is the object 'you' (classical form of تو + را), and باد و گرد is the subject of یابد in inverted order meaning 'wind and dust' (i.e. the elements that may overtake you).
segments.s040-g75: چو آیی = 'when you come' where چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' and آیی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of 'to come' used for the temporal subordinate. به کاخ فریدون is the destination phrase 'to Fereydun's palace' (به + noun + name). فرود is a noun/verb meaning 'descent; alighting' used here as a directive to dismount/descend on arrival.
segments.s040-g76: نخستین is an adverb 'first'. ز is the poetic/prepositional 'of/from' used here to mark the group targeted (classical usage instead of modern به), هر دو پسر means 'each / both of the two sons', ده is the imperative 'give' (omitted from the provided word list but present in the line), and درود is the object 'a greeting' — together: 'first give a greeting to each of the two sons.'
segments.s040-g77: پس آنگه are consecutive temporal/transition particles ('then' / 'thereupon') introducing the instruction. بگویش is the imperative/reporting form 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic -ش 'to him'). که introduces the reported clause; ترس خدای is a genitive phrase 'fear of God' (ترس = fear, خدای = God).
segments.s040-g78: بباید is the modal 'must/ought' and که again introduces the subordinate clause. باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' here meaning 'should be / must be'. به هر دو سرای is a prepositional phrase 'in/between both houses/dwellings' (به + هر دو + سرای); note that the lemma given for سرای in the data does not match the sense 'dwelling' so that sense must be treated separately.
segments.s040-g79: جوان را بود is a topical existential/past construction: 'for the young man there was' (i.e. 'the young man had'). روز پیری literally 'the day/time of old age' = 'in old age', and امید is the noun 'hope' — together: 'the young man had the hope for the day of old age.'
segments.s040-g80: نگردد is a negative-present/subjunctive form of 'گردیدن' meaning 'may not / should not become' (here dependent on the prior 'hope'). The phrase سیه موی (poetic word order: adjective before noun) means 'black-haired' (سیه = black, موی = hair), and گشته سپید is a perfect-participial description 'having become white' (گشته = become/been turned, سپید = white). Put together: 'that the black hair would not become white.'
segments.s040-g137: نشاندش is a past transitive verb (نشاند) with the clitic object -ش = “him” (so: “he seated him”). In classical/poetic word order the verb can precede the subject; هم آنگه (هم = “also/too”, آنگه = “then”) is an adverbial phrase placed before the subject فریدون. ز پای is a short prepositional phrase (“from/at the foot of / at the feet”), placed after the subject in this verse order.
segments.s040-g138: سزاوار here is an adjective meaning “worthy/deserving” used with the verb کردن (کردش = کرد + -ش “him”) to mean “made him deserving.” بر خویش جای is a prepositional/nominal phrase: بر = “on/upon,” خوی + ش = خویش “his own” (the -ش is the possessive clitic), جای = “place/position,” so “made him deserving of a place at his own (side/seat).”
segments.s040-g139: بپرسیدش is the polite plural/imparative stem بپرسید plus the object clitic -ش “him,” functioning as an instruction: “ask him.” از marks the topic/concern here (“about/regarding”), دو گرامی نخست = “the two honored/esteemed first ones” (گرامی = “honored,” نخست = “first/foremost”).
segments.s040-g140: که introduces a relative clause “who/that.” هستند is the present/plural copula “they are.” شادان دل is a compact attribute: شادان modifies دل (literally “joyful of heart”); و connects and تن‌درست is a compound adjective meaning “healthy/sound in body.” The clause describes the two nobles.
segments.s040-g141: دگر گفت is a short clause where دگر has the sense “again/further” (he said again or another person then said) and گفت is the past verb “said.” کز is the archaic contracted conjunction (که + از) introducing a causal/explanatory clause here (“that/from”), followed by راه دور و دراز “a road distant and long” (adjectives following the noun in poetic order).
segments.s040-g142: شدی is the past of شدن (2nd person singular “you became” or ‘you have become’). رنجه is a predicative/adjectival participle meaning “distressed/afflicted” (“you became distressed”). اندر introduces the locative “in/within,” and نشیب و فراز are paired nouns “descent(s) and ascent(s)” — idiomatically “the lows and highs/ups and downs” through which the person suffered.
segments.s040-g143: فرستاده گفت is a simple reporting clause: فرستاده “the messenger/sent one” is the subject and گفت is the past verb “said.” ای گرانمایه شاه is a vocative: ای = “O,” گرانمایه = “precious/esteemed,” شاه = “king.”
segments.s040-g144: This verbless-appearance clause follows poetical word order: ابی is an adjective (literally “blue/azure” in the listed senses, used here as a predicate or descriptive epithet before تو), تو is the 2nd-person pronoun “you,” and مبیناد is an archaic/poetic form meaning “is seen/appears.” کس پیش‌گاه = “anyone before the court/presence.” Taken together the line reads as an address: “O esteemed king — you appear (thus), and no one comes before the court,” i.e. “no one appears before your presence.”
segments.s040-g153: بفرمود is an honorific past form meaning 'he said/commanded'. پس ('then') orders the sequence. تا introduces a subordinate clause here in the sense 'that/so that' (not temporal 'until') and links to the verb phrase زبان برگشاد—'open his tongue'—where برگشاد is a perfective/jussive-like form meaning 'open' (i.e. 'that he open his tongue'). The word order is typical Classical Persian: verb (بفرمود) + connective (پس تا) + subordinate noun phrase (زبان برگشاد).
segments.s040-g154: شنیده is a participial/past form 'having heard'. سخن 'speech' is the theme; the doubled idiomatic سر به سر ('from head to head') means 'entirely/throughout' and is expressed by the two سر tokens placed around به. کرد is the simple past auxiliary/verb 'did/made' used here to produce 'recounted/caused to be remembered', and یاد functions as 'memory/from memory' — together: 'having heard, (he) made/recited the whole speech from memory.'
segments.s040-g155: فریدون is the proper name. بدو is the prepositional form 'to him/toward him'. پهن modifies the following verb as 'wide/broad'. بگشاد here is the verb 'opened' (literally 'made open'), so the phrase means 'opened (his) ear wide toward him' — i.e. he pricked up or spread his ear to listen. Word order: Name + indirect object + adverb/adjective + verb + object (گوش).
segments.s040-g156: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = 'he heard'). مغزش 'his brain' (مغز + possessive -ش), برآمد is 'arose/rose up' and the predicate به جوش is a metaphor 'into a boil' — figuratively 'surged/was inflamed'. Together: 'when he heard, his brain rose into a boil' = 'his mind surged with passion/ardor.'
segments.s040-g157: فرستاده را گفت is 'he said to the messenger' — را marks the definite object (here the addressee). گفت is the verb 'said' (past). کای is the vocative 'O' and هوشیار is the adjective/noun 'watchful, sensible', addressing the messenger politely. Word order: indirect object marked + verb, then the quoted vocative.
segments.s040-g158: بباید is a modal 'it is necessary / must' and takes the 2nd-person clitic ت (ت) to indicate 'you must'. را here functions as the clitic attached to that pronoun (forming 'ترا' = 'you'). پوزش means 'pardon/apology'. اکنون 'now' and به کار is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'to be of use/necessary' — so the whole clause: 'you must (ترا) now make/offer apology' (i.e. 'you must now apologize'). The verb is implied by the modal construction.
segments.s040-g159: که here functions as causal 'for/because'. من 'I'. چشم 'eye' with از ایشان 'on/for them' = 'I had an eye on them' (i.e. I watched/regarded them). چنین 'thus/so' qualifies how he watched. داشتم is the past imperfect 'I had/was having' — 'I had (kept) my eye on them thus.'
segments.s040-g160: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle 'always/continually'. بر دل خویش is literal 'upon my heart' (بر = on; دل = heart; خویش = his/her/own with ش as possessive). بگذاشتم is past 'I placed/put' (often 'kept' or 'laid to heart') — together: 'I would continually place/keep it upon my heart' = 'I kept it in my heart always.'
segments.s040-g89: Literal order: numeral + noun + past copula + adjectives. بودت is the archaic/poetic 2nd-person singular past of بودن ('you were' / 'you had' in this context). سه فرزند بودت = 'you had three children.' خردمند و گرد are coordinate adjectives describing those children; گرد here is poetic and its precise nuance is context-dependent.
segments.s040-g90: بزرگ آمدت is a verbal clause: آمدت is past tense with enclitic -ت 'to you' (= 'came to you' / 'arrived for you'), and بزرگ is the adjective modifying the (implicit) son. The following three words (تیره بیدار خرد) are a string of epithets — adjectives/nouns placed after the verb phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s040-g91: ندیدی is negative past 'you did not see' (classical poetic past). هنر here means 'skill/virtue' and با یکی بیشتر literally 'more with one' = 'greater in one (than in another).' The comparative idea is expressed by با ... بیشتر ('with one, more').
segments.s040-g92: This clause begins with کجا used as 'where / in what place'. دیگری 'the other/another' is the subject; ز (here enclitic z + او = 'زو') = 'from/of him' (poetic 'از'). فرو برد is a compound verbal idea: فرو (down) + برد (took/put) = 'lowered/put down' (i.e., 'bowed'), and سر = 'head' is the object. Word order is inverted but typical for poetry.
segments.s040-g93: یکی را with را marks a definite object 'one (of them)'. دم اژدها is a noun phrase 'the tail (or tip) of the dragon' and ساختی is the 2nd-person past 'you made' / 'you fashioned'. The verb comes at the end, as in Persian syntax.
segments.s040-g94: Again یکی را is the definite object. به ابر اندر is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the cloud' (به = to/into; اندر = in/within, reinforcing the locative). افراختی is the 2nd-person past 'you raised/erected/exalted' and sits at the clause end.
segments.s040-g95: یکی را (one) is again marked by را as definite object/target. تاج بر سر is a fixed prepositional phrase 'a crown on (the) head.' ببالین تو is an adverbial 'at your bedside / close beside you' (ببالین = 'at the bedside of'), and تو is the vocative/address 'you' (poetic usage).
segments.s040-g96: برو is an imperative or exhortation 'go/come forth' used poetically as 'be off / go on.' شاد گشته is a perfect/periphrastic result: شاد 'happy' + گشته (perfect participle 'has become') = 'has become happy' / 'be glad.' جهان‌بین تو is a vocative apposition 'O seer of the world (you).' The clause addresses the addressee and tells them to be glad because of what has happened.
segments.s040-g97: The clause uses a paired negation with نه ... کمتریم: کمتریم (first‑person plural comparative) means 'we are less' but the leading نه negates it: 'we are not less.' زو is the archaic literary contraction of از او ('than him' / 'from him') and به مام و پدر is a prepositional phrase 'with respect to mother and father' (i.e. 'in mother or father'). Word order is subject (ما) + comparison (زو) + metric phrase (به مام و پدر) + predicate (کمتریم).
segments.s040-g98: Another parallel negative: نه ... نه introduces two coordinated negations. بر تخت شاهی is a locative phrase 'on the royal throne' (بر + تخت + adjectival شاهی). اندر خوریم places the action 'eat/dine' inside the court: خوریم is 1st‑person plural present 'we eat' (here 'we do not partake at court').
segments.s040-g99: A vocative/exclamatory address: ایا here functions like 'O' or 'indeed' calling attention. دادگر is used as a substantive/adjectival title 'the just one' or 'just ruler,' شهریار names the sovereign and زمین 'the earth' specifies his realm: 'O just sovereign of the earth.'
segments.s040-g100: برین is the fused literary form of بر + این ('for/on this' → 'for this'); داد here is the noun 'justice.' هرگز مباد is a fixed optative/negative formula 'may it never be' (مباد = 'may it not be'), and آفرین functions as 'praise/acclamation' (object of the wish). The whole clause expresses a wish that 'praise for this justice may never occur.'
segments.s040-g101: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). تاج is the subject 'the crown.' از آن تارک is an older phrasing meaning 'with respect to / for that تارک' (تارک = one who has abandoned / a deserter or 'the one who left'), and بی‑بها is an adjective 'without price / priceless' used here to mean lacking proper worth. The phrase sets up the condition 'if the crown becomes (for) that deserter worthless/without value.'
segments.s040-g102: شود is the 3rd‑person singular of شدن 'become' (marks the outcome of the condition). The clause that follows uses classical word order: subject (جهان) follows the verb یابد ('finds') — here یابد means 'finds' or 'comes to be for' — and زو (archaic for از او) + رها expresses 'free/from him.' Together the second half says that the world will be found free of him (i.e. the world will be rid of him).
segments.s040-g103: سپاری is from سپردن and here functions as an imperative/optative 'entrust/leave' (i.e. 'give' or 'assign'). بدو = 'to him.' گوشه‌ای از جهان is the object 'a corner/part of the world' (گوشه‌ای = 'a corner/part', از جهان = 'of the world').
segments.s040-g104: نشیند (3rd‑person singular) here is hortative/optative 'let him sit' or simple 'he will sit.' چو ما 'like us' compares his state to the speaker's; از تو خسته 'weary/tired of you' is a causa/agent phrase ('tired of you'), and نهان 'hidden' qualifies his sitting. The line says: let him sit in a corner, hidden like us and tired of you.
segments.s040-g41: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase meaning “well” (به + خوبی); شنیده is a past/perfect participle/past-form “heard” placed after the adverbial; همه is the direct object placed before the light-verb construction یاد کرد. یاد کرد is the verbalized noun construction (یاد + کرد) meaning “remembered” where کرد is the past 3rd-sg auxiliary/lexical verb.
segments.s040-g42: سر تور is a noun phrase meaning “the head of Tūr” (possessive relation expressed by adjacency, as is common in poetic Persian); بی‌مغز is an adjective after that noun meaning “brainless”; پرباد is a predicate adjective “full of wind / windy”; کرد is a past-tense causative/lexical verb “made” — together: “(he) made Tūr’s brainless head full of wind.”
segments.s040-g43: چو is a temporal subordinating conjunction “when”; این راز is the object “this secret”; بشنید is the past 3rd-singular “he heard”; تور دلیر is the subject with an epithet placed after the name (poetic inversion), “Tūr the brave.”
segments.s040-g44: برآشفت is past 3rd-singular “became angry/was enraged”; ناگاه is an adverb “suddenly” modifying that verb; برسان is a jussive/imperative-like or causative form meaning “bring/send (for)” (here: he ordered that the lion be brought); شیر is the direct object “lion.” The clause thus narrates a sudden enraged reaction and an order to fetch the lion.
segments.s040-g45: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted poetic order meaning “thus (چنین) he gave (داد) an answer (پاسخ)” — داد is the past 3rd-singular verb and پاسخ is the object placed before it; که introduces the reported content (‘that’), and با شهریار indicates the addressee/recipient relation (with/to the king).
segments.s040-g46: بگو is an imperative or hortative “say/tell” (addressing a messenger); این سخن is the direct object “this speech/these words”; هم‌چنین is a particle meaning “thus/likewise”; یاد دار is an idiomatic imperative-like phrase ‘keep in memory/remember’ formed from the noun یاد plus an abbreviated/poetic form دار (elliptical for ‘have/hold’) — together: “Tell the king this, and keep it in mind likewise.”
segments.s040-g47: که introduces the reported clause ‘that’; ما را is the patient/object “us” marked by the object marker را; به گاه جوانی پدر is a temporal prepositional phrase “at the time of the father’s youth / when our father was young” (به + گاه = “at the time of”; جوانی پدر = “the father’s youth”).
segments.s040-g48: بدین گونه is an idiomatic fixed phrase “in this way / in this manner” (بدین = archaic/poetic form for به + این; گونه = manner); بفریفت is past 3rd-singular “deceived” (he deceived); ای دادگر is a vocative “O just one / O just ruler,” addressing someone with the epithet دادگر. The whole segment is the speaker’s complaint: ‘he deceived us in this way, O just one!’
segments.s040-g33: چو here is the conditional/temporal particle 'if/when' introducing the condition. The list that follows — ایران و دشت یلان و یمن — is a coordinated series of place-nouns: 'Iran', 'dāšt-e yelān' (the plain/wilderness of the yelān, i.e. warriors/heroes), and 'Yemen'. The conjunction و simply links the items in normal Persian coordination order.
segments.s040-g34: This clause contains the verb دهد (3rd person singular present/subjunctive/future-like classical form 'he gives') with two recipient phrases: به ایرج (to Iraj) and (ellipsis:) روم و خاور به من (Rome and the East to me). Word order is slightly inverted/elliptical: the verb governs both parts, so the sense is 'if he gives X to Iraj, (let him give) Rome and the East to me.'
segments.s040-g35: سپارد is 3rd-person singular (classical present/future sense) 'he entrusts / will entrust'. ترا is the object 'تو' with the accusative clitic -را (here written split), so 'to you' / 'you (as recipient)'. مرز ترکان و چین is the thing being entrusted: 'the frontier/border of the Turks and China', with و linking the two peoples/regions.
segments.s040-g36: که introduces a subordinate clause of result/purpose ('that / so that'). از تو literally 'from you' here means 'to be from you' or 'to come out of you' (i.e. the origin). سپهدار is a nominal title 'commander' and ایران‌زمین 'the land/realm of Iran'. The clause omits an explicit copula (e.g. باشد) — this nominal clause expresses the hoped-for/resulting appointment: 'that the commander of Iran be/from you.'
segments.s040-g37: بدین بخشش = 'by/with this grant/allotment' (بدین = به + این; بخشش = grant/donation). اندر مرا پای نیست is an archaic word order: literally 'in / for me foot is not' → 'I have no footing / standing in it.' پای نیست functions as an existential negative 'there is no footing/claim (for me)'.
segments.s040-g38: به مغز پدر اندرون رای نیست: به + مغز پدر اندرون = 'within the father's mind/inner breast'. رای نیست = 'there is no opinion/plan' — رای here has the sense 'plan/counsel'. The whole clause states a lack of any intention or counsel in the father's heart; again no copula is required in classical Persian.
segments.s040-g39: هیون is a wild/monstrous beast; فرستاده here is a past-participial/ adjectival use 'sent' (literally 'the beast sent'). بگزارد is the classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive/presentive form of گزار 'to place' with the prefix ب-; پای is its object 'foot'. The clause means 'the sent beast placed its foot / set foot.'
segments.s040-g40: بیامد is the past form 'came' (or 'it came'); به نزدیک = 'to/near' (locative/towards), توران is the place-name 'Turan', and خدای here is an exclamatory vocative 'O God!' The sequence says the beast 'came up near Turan' with an interjection of alarm at the end.
segments.s040-g193: This pre-verbal cluster names the affected party and what was taken: شما را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker attached to شما (you) to mark possession/affectedness (literally “your…”). کنون‌گر is an adverbial/temporal qualifier “now, at present.” دل از راه من literally ‘the heart from my way’ uses از to mark separation (‘from’) and من as the possessor after راه (my way). The whole phrase functions as the object phrase that the following verb will act on.
segments.s040-g194: This is the verb phrase showing direction/result: به + noun expresses motion/turning into (‘into’/‘towards’). کژی و تاری are two abstract nouns joined by و (and): ‘crookedness and darkness.’ کشید is the simple past 3rd-person singular of کشیدن ‘to draw/pull’ (here ‘drew/has drawn’); note the verb appears before its subject for poetic effect. اهرمن (Ahriman/the Devil) is the agent following the verb.
segments.s040-g195: ببینید is an address to the audience (imperative or hortative ‘look/see/attend’). تا here introduces the content clause meaning ‘that/so that’ rather than temporal ‘until.’ کردگار بلند is a noun + adjective order meaning ‘the lofty Creator’ (adjective follows noun in Persian).
segments.s040-g196: چنین ‘thus/so’ qualifies the action; از شما ‘from you’ marks the source/agent of what is approved. The sequence کرد خواهد پسند is a poetic/inverted ordering of the verbal complex (equivalent to پسند خواهد کرد ‘will (come to) like/approve’). کرد here functions as part of that verbal phrase and پسند is the notion of approval—together indicating that the Creator will approve/accept such from you.
segments.s040-g197: یکی داستان گویم is ‘I will tell one tale’ with یکی (a/one) + داستان (tale). گویم is 1st-person present/subjunctive form of گفتن used here for ‘I will tell/I tell.’ ار بشنوید is a conditional clause ‘if you hear/listen’ (ار = اگر ‘if’). The condition precedes the consequence in the next segment.
segments.s040-g198: همان ‘the very thing’ / ‘that same’ begins this result clause. بر که here introduces a relative/content clause ‘that which’ (بر functioning as ‘upon/with respect to’). کارید is 2nd-person plural (you) form from کاشتن ‘to plant/sow’ (‘you have sown’ or ‘you sow’ depending on aspect). خود (self) emphasizes the actor (yourselves). بدروید is a poetic verb meaning ‘you will reap/harvest’ — literal reconstruction is ‘you will reap what you planted yourselves’ (the verb is forward-looking/resultative).
segments.s040-g199: چنین گفت introduces direct speech (‘thus said’). باما means ‘to/with us’ (i.e., he spoke to us). سخن رهنمای literally ‘the speech of the guide’ (سخن = words/speech, رهنمای = guide); noun phrases follow normal Persian order (noun + modifier), but poetic word-order may be slightly inverted.
segments.s040-g200: جزین ‘except this’ is fronted for emphasis. است is the copula ‘is.’ جاوید ‘everlasting/eternal’ functions as the predicate adjective. ما را سرای = ‘our house/dwelling’ where ما را marks the possessor (poetic inversion compared with the more neutral سرای ما). The whole clause says that, apart from this, our abode is eternal.
segments.s040-g113: The phrase is a prepositional + noun phrase 'به درگاه شاه آفریدون' ('to the court of King Afrīdūn') followed by the past-tense verb 'رسید' ('arrived/reached'). Word order is typical Persian: locative phrase before the finite verb.
segments.s040-g114: 'برآورده‌ای' functions as an indefinite/participle phrase meaning 'one brought/placed (in)', followed by the past-tense verb 'دید' ('he saw'). The clause 'سر ناپدید' is a verbless nominal phrase 'head (was) vanished' — in Persian poetry the copula is omitted, so it reads 'he saw (one) brought in; (the) head (was) vanished.'
segments.s040-g115: 'به ابر اندر آورده بالای او' is a locative+verb cluster: 'به ابر اندر آورده' = 'brought into the cloud(s)' (literally 'into the cloud brought'), with the slightly unusual word order typical of epic verse. 'بالای او' ('above him') is an adjunct specifying the vertical position.
segments.s040-g116: This is an inverted nominal phrase: 'زمین کوه تا کوه' = 'the land/earth, from mountain to mountain' and 'پهنای او' = 'his breadth/width.' The genitive relation ('his breadth' describing how wide he is) appears after the descriptive span ('mountain to mountain'), a common poetic order.
segments.s040-g117: 'نشسته' is a participial/adjectival predicate 'seated,' followed by locative phrases: 'به در' = 'at the door/threshold' and 'بر گرانمایگان' = 'before/upon the illustrious ones' (here 'بر' marks position in relation to the nobles). The whole phrase describes someone's posture and position relative to the nobles.
segments.s040-g118: 'به پرده درون' is a locative phrase 'within the curtain' (به + noun + درون), and 'جای پرمایگان' is a noun + genitive: 'the place/seat of the great ones.' The order places the locative phrase before the possessed noun phrase, as is common in verse.
segments.s040-g119: Literal order: 'به یک دست' ('in/at one hand') marks location, followed by 'بربسته' ('bound/restrained') qualifying the animals 'شیر و پلنگ' ('lion and leopard'). In Persian the adjective/participle 'بربسته' can appear immediately before or after the nouns it modifies in poetry, yielding 'in one hand (were) bound — a lion and a leopard.'
segments.s040-g120: 'به دست دگر' = 'in the other hand'; 'ژنده' is an adjective 'tattered/ragged' modifying 'پیلان' (plural of 'پیل', 'elephants'), and 'جنگ' ('of war') qualifies them as 'war‑elephants.' The phrase therefore reads 'in the other hand, the tattered war‑elephants' — a compact, poetic description where adjectives and qualifiers stack before the noun.
segments.s040-g185: همه راستی functions as a unit: همه intensifies راستی (meaning 'complete/whole truth'), and خواستم is the past first‑person 'I wanted/wished'. زین is an archaic/formal prepositional form (here 'از این' = 'in/from this') that links to the following noun سخن ('speech/words'). Word order is typical older Persian: intensifier + noun + verb, then the locative/relative phrase zīn s̱ukhan.
segments.s040-g186: به کژی is a prepositional phrase ('with regard to / toward crookedness'). The clause uses correlative negation نه ... نه ... ('neither ... nor ...') with the verb بود (past 'was') and predicate پیداست (here split: پیدا نه بود = 'was not apparent'). سر and بن are parallel nouns meaning 'top/head' and 'root/foundation', respectively; their order emphasizes 'no trace anywhere, neither at the top nor at the root.'
segments.s040-g187: همه is the universal quantifier 'everyone/all'. ترس یزدان is a genitive phrase 'fear of God'; the adjective بد here functions as an intensifier in poetic usage ('great/severe'), and اندر میان is a locative 'in the midst (among them)'. The copula ('may be') is omitted, an optative/poetic ellipsis common in Persian (i.e. 'may fear of God be among all').
segments.s040-g188: Again همه راستی is 'complete truth' with همه qualifying راستی; خواستم is past first‑person 'I wished/desired'. در جهان is a locative phrase 'in/throughout the world'. The sentence repeats the speaker's desire (خواستم) and locates it globally.
segments.s040-g189: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آباد qualifies گیتی ('the world made prosperous' or 'the prosperous world'); دادند is past plural 'they gave' (impersonal/generically 'they' or 'people in power'), and به من marks the recipient 'to me'. Word order places the temporal clause up front: 'When they gave the prosperous world to me...'.
segments.s040-g190: نجستم here is a negated past of جستن (the initial n- is the negative: 'ن + جستم' = 'I did not seek/try'). It takes the following infinitive/masdar phrase پراگندن انجمن as its object: 'to scatter the assembly'. پراگندن is the infinitive 'to scatter/spread' and انجمن is the object 'assembly/council'.
segments.s040-g191: مگر here functions adversatively as 'but rather' (contrasting what follows with the previous action). همچنان means 'in the same way' or 'thus/so', and گفتم is the past first‑person 'I said/decided' (classical Persian often uses 'گفتم' to introduce a resolution or statement). آباد modifies تخت ('prosperous throne').
segments.s040-g192: سپارم is a present/subjunctive form of سپردن here used as the complement of گفتم: 'I said that I would entrust/leave (it)'. به marks the indirect object, and سه دیده نیک‌بخت is a compound noun phrase 'the three (سه) دیده (literally 'eyed', i.e. 'those of sight') نیک‌بخت (fortunate ones)'; in context this means 'the three fortunate persons' or 'the three lucky heirs'. Note the adjective نیک‌بخت follows the noun, a common Persian order.
segments.s040-g217: بجویید is an imperative/hortative plural (prefix بــ + verb) meaning 'seek' or 'look for'. The coordinating و links this command to the next clause. آن توشه ره کنید is a small clause where آن توشه ('that provision') is the object and ره کنید ('make/prepare for the road') is the verb; توشه and ره form the collocation توشهٔ راه 'provisions for the road' with کنید as the action word.
segments.s040-g218: بکوشید is another imperative/hortative plural 'strive' or 'make an effort'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'). رنج کوته کنید is a causative periphrasis: رنج (object 'suffering') + adjective کوته ('short') followed by کرد‌ن (کنید) meaning 'make/bring about' — i.e. 'shorten suffering'.
segments.s040-g219: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. بشنید is a past 3rd-person form 'he heard'. گفتار اوی is the object phrase 'his speech/words' with اوی an older possessive form 'his/her' attached to گفتار; the whole line reads 'the messenger heard his words'.
segments.s040-g220: زمین را uses را as the definite object marker: 'the ground/earth'. ببوسید is past 3rd-person 'he kissed'. The conjunction و links another verb: برگاشت (past 3rd) + روی ('face') — here روی serves as the object/target (without را, a common poetic omission), so برگاشت روی means 'he lifted/turned his face' or 'he turned his face back'.
segments.s040-g221: ز پیش is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from before' (ز = poetic از, پیش = 'before / in the presence of'); it specifies source/location. فریدون is the proper name. چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying بازگشت, which is the past verb 'he returned'. The clause therefore means 'he returned from Fereydun's presence in such a way'.
segments.s040-g222: The first که introduces the result/comparison clause ('so ... that'). گفتی here functions idiomatically ('one would say' / 'as it is said' — a conventional poetic formula) rather than a straightforward 2nd-person past. The second که again introduces the content of that saying: با باد 'with the wind' + انباز (an archaic/adverbial/formulaic word) and گشت (past 'went/moved') — together 'he went/was gone with the wind.' The doubled که is a standard connective in such comparative/result constructions.
segments.s040-g129: بفرمود is a third-person past honorific verb (‘he commanded/said’). تا here introduces the immediate result/sequence (‘so/thereupon’). پرده برداشتند is a past plural clause: پرده ‘the curtain’ + برداشتند ‘they lifted/removed it.’ The whole segment therefore means ‘he ordered, and they lifted the curtain.’
segments.s040-g130: This clause gives what they did with the horse. بر introduces the direct thing being placed (here the steed), اسپش is the possessive form ‘his steed/horse,’ ز درگاه is a locative phrase ‘at/from the threshold (court door)’ with ز as the poetic preposition, and بگذاشتند is the past plural ‘they placed/left.’ Together: ‘they placed his steed at the threshold.’
segments.s040-g131: چو here is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ چشمش ‘his eye’ (possessive), به ... روی ‘on/upon the face of’ Fereydun (به + روی + proper name), and رسید is the past ‘reached’/‘fell upon’ — a common Persian idiom meaning ‘his gaze fell on Fereydun.’
segments.s040-g132: This is the result the speaker observed: implicit subject ‘he’ + دید ‘saw.’ همه modifies دیده و دل (‘every eye and heart’). پر ز is the compound expression ‘full of’ (پر + ز), شاه is the thing they are full of. Literally: ‘he saw that every eye and heart was full of the king.’
segments.s040-g133: به بالای سرو is a comparative/descriptive phrase ‘to the height/of the cypress’ (indicating tallness). و connects the next simile; چو خورشید روی means ‘with a face like the sun’ (چو introduces a simile). The phrase gives two parallel epithets: stature and face.
segments.s040-g134: This is a compact poetic simile. چو introduces ‘like’; کافور (camphor) + گرد (‘around/about’) + گل سرخ موی is a genitive/attributive chain ‘the red-rose of (his) hair’ — so the structure reads ‘like camphor around the red-rose of hair,’ i.e. his hair looked like camphor encircling a red rose. Classical word order stacks the elements for imagery.
segments.s040-g135: دولب here is the fused classical spelling of دو لب (‘two lips’). پر ز + خنده = ‘full of laughter.’ Then دو رخ ‘two cheeks’ (both cheeks) + پر ز شرم = ‘full of modesty/shame.’ The phrase strings parallel epithets (lips/cheeks) using پر ز to mark ‘filled with.’
segments.s040-g136: کیانی qualifies زبان (literally ‘Kayanian tongue’ — used adjectivally as ‘royal’). زبان پر ز گفتار نرم = ‘a tongue full of gentle speech’: پر ز marks ‘full of,’ گفتار نرم is a noun + adjective (‘gentle/soft speech’). The whole is another epithet emphasizing polite, graceful speech.
segments.s040-g177: 'بدان' here functions as an oathal prepositional phrase ('by that/with that') introducing what follows; the sequence 'برترین نام یزدان پاک' is a noun phrase: 'برترین' is a superlative adjective ('the most/supreme') modifying 'نام' ('name'), and 'یزدان پاک' is a postpositive qualificatory phrase 'the pure God' (classical/poetic term 'یزدان' = God). Word order is adjective + noun + divine epithet, typical in Persian poetic style.
segments.s040-g178: This is a pair of prepositional phrases joined by 'و'. 'به رخشنده خورشید' = 'by/on/at the radiant sun' where 'رخشنده' is an adjectival present-participle 'radiant' modifying 'خورشید' ('sun'); 'بر تیره خاک' = 'upon the dark earth' with 'بر' as the preposition 'upon' and 'تیره' an adjective 'dark'. The two phrases form parallel objects of the oathal 'بدان' in the previous segment.
segments.s040-g179: This is a series of oathal prepositional phrases introduced by 'به' (= 'by'/'upon'): 'تخت و کلاه' = 'throne and crown/helmet' and 'به ناهید و ماه' = 'by Anahita (the goddess/Venus) and the moon'. The repeated 'به' marks each pair as things sworn by; nouns are coordinated with 'و'.
segments.s040-g180: 'که' introduces the explanatory clause 'that...'; 'من' is the subject 'I'. 'بد' is an adjective/adverb meaning 'evil' or 'bad' modifying the action; 'نکردم' is the negated past tense first-person singular of 'کردن' ('I did not do'). 'شما را' is the direct object marked by the object particle 'را' ('you'), and 'نگاه' is a noun 'gaze/look' (so literal structure: 'that I did not do an evil look to you' → 'I did not cast an evil glance at you').
segments.s040-g181: 'یکی انجمن کردم' = 'I made/convened an assembly' where 'یکی' functions as an indefinite/indefinite-article-like word ('a certain' / 'one'), 'انجمن' is the noun 'assembly/meeting', and 'کردم' is the past first-person 'I made/held'. 'از بخردان' is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the wise', with 'از' marking the group whose members make up the assembly.
segments.s040-g182: 'ستاره شناسان' is a compound meaning 'star‑knowers' = 'astronomers' (ستاره 'star' + شناسان, an agentive/plural form), 'و هم موبدان' adds 'and also the mobeds' (موبدان = Zoroastrian priests). 'هم' here means 'also/together with'. The whole segment names the participants of the assembly.
segments.s040-g183: 'بسی روزگاران شدست اندرین' is a poetic past/perfect expression: 'بسی روزگاران' = 'many ages/times', 'شدست' is an archaic compound meaning 'has become / have passed' (شد + است contracted), and 'اندرین' = 'in this / in these [matters]'. Together: 'many ages have passed in this'.
segments.s040-g184: The clause 'نکردیم بر باد بخشش زمین' has negative past verb 'نکردیم' ('we did not do / we did not allow'). 'بر باد' ('to the wind') combines idiomatically with a verbal idea to mean 'to let something be wasted/ruined', and 'بخشش زمین' = 'the grant/donation of the land' (بخشش = grant/donation, زمین = land). So literal order: 'we did not (let) the grant of the land go to the wind' → 'we did not squander the land's grant.'
segments.s040-g209: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/for'); چون functions as a temporal 'as/when'; آز is the noun 'greed'; گردد (گَرْدَد) is the 3rd-person singular present/epistolary form of 'شدن' meaning 'become/arise'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; دلها is the plural 'hearts'; تهی means 'devoid/empty (of)'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: verb (گردد) follows its complement phrase ('آز') and the phrase 'ز دلها تهی' states the resultant state ('empty of hearts').
segments.s040-g210: چه here is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what' (rhetorical); آن is the demonstrative 'that' repeated for emphasis; خاک 'earth/soil'; و 'and'; آن again 'that'; تاج 'crown' and شاهنشهی (شاهنشاهی) qualifies تاج as 'of kingship/imperial rule'. Persian poetry often omits the copula ('is/are'), so the clause literally lists 'that earth and that crown of empire' as the object of the rhetorical 'what'.
segments.s040-g211: کسی کو is an older/poetic relative construction meaning 'someone who'; برادر is the direct object 'brother'; فروشد (from فروختن) here functions as a present/simple verb 'sells'; به خاک 'for the earth/dust' means 'for something worthless' (betraying for a trivial gain). Word order places the relative particle after 'کسی' and the verb before the prepositional phrase, as common in older Persian.
segments.s040-g212: سزد‌گر is the fused conditional/optative 'سزد گر' = 'it is fitting/proper that'; نخوانندش is 3rd-person plural negative form of خواندن with the object suffix -ش 'him' so 'they should not call/read/recognize him' (here 'call/consider him'); از introduces the standard idiom 'از آب پاک' = 'of/purified by clean water' where آب پاک stands for ritual/ moral purity. Together the clause says it is proper that such a man not be deemed pure by clean water.
segments.s040-g213: جهان 'the world' is the subject; چون here functions as 'as/when'; شما 'you' is the object; دید is the past 'saw/has seen' and و بیند pairs it with بیند (present/subjunctive/gnomic 'sees' — a poetic mix of tenses meaning 'has seen and sees'); بسی modifies بیند as 'many/much'. The clause means the world has seen you and sees many like you.
segments.s040-g214: نخواهد here is the negative future/auxiliary 'will not'; شدن (infinitive of شدن) completes the periphrastic future 'will not become'; رام 'tame' is the predicate adjective; با هر کسی 'with/by any person' gives the agent/instrument. Together the phrase means 'will not become tame (i.e., be subdued or satisfied) by every/any person.'
segments.s040-g215: کزین combines کِه/ز + این to mean 'from this/of this'; هر چه دانید is a free relative 'whatever you know' (دانید here addressing 'you' in present/subjunctive or indicative); از کردگار 'of/from the Creator' specifies the domain of knowledge. The unit means 'of all this, whatever you know about God'.
segments.s040-g216: بود is the copula/auxiliary 'is/was/will be' (here best read as 'will be' or 'there is'); رستگاری 'salvation' is the predicate; به‌روز شمار is a compound locative 'on the day of counting/reckoning' (i.e. the Day of Judgment). The clause states that salvation will be at that day of account.
segments.s040-g201: به تخت خرد بر نشست — literal order: preposition + noun phrase + verbal phrase. به + تخت خرد = 'to/onto the throne of wisdom' (here 'به' marks direction/position: 'on/onto'); بر نشست is a compound verbal idea 'sat upon' (بر = 'upon', نشست = 'sat/settled'). آزتان is the subject (with possessive suffix -تان 'your') placed after the verb for poetic inversion: 'your greed sat upon the throne of wisdom.'
segments.s040-g202: چرا شد چنین دیو انبازتان — چرا شد introduces a question 'why did ... become/ happen'; چنین 'such' modifies دیو 'demon/monster'. شد is the past/become (here 'did become'). انبازتان is a possessive noun with the plural/2nd‑person suffix -تان ('your anbāz'); انباز is an archaic/obscure epithet and is left uninterpreted here (poetic or rare word).
segments.s040-g203: بترسم که در چنگ این اژدها — بترسم is 1st person singular present/subjunctive of ترسیدن, here used idiomatically 'I fear (that)...' and is followed by the conjunction که 'that' to introduce the subordinate clause. در چنگ این اژدها = 'in the claw/grip of this dragon', where در + noun yields the locative 'in/within the grip'.
segments.s040-g204: روان یابد از کالبدتان رها — literal order is inverted for poetic effect. روان 'the soul' is the logical subject; یابد is a form of یافتن used optatively/subjunctively here ('may find' / 'may come to'), so the clause expresses a feared outcome. از کالبدتان 'from your body' (کالبد + possessive -تان). رها functions as predicate/adjective 'loosed/freed' (i.e. 'the soul may be freed').
segments.s040-g205: مرا خود ز گیتی گه رفتن است — مرا (here split in the word list) functions as a dative/experiential 'for me' (emphatic by being followed by خود 'myself'). ز گیتی = 'from the world' (poetic 'ز' for 'from'). گه رفتن 'the time/occasion of going' (گه = occasion/time; رفتن = departure, here poetically = dying/leaving). است is the copula: 'it is the time for me to go from the world.'
segments.s040-g206: نه هنگام تندی و آشفتن است — نه negates the clause ('not'); هنگام تندی و آشفتن است = 'it is the time of haste and disturbance' with هنگام 'time' governing the abstract nouns تندی 'haste' and آشفتن 'disorder/upset'. The whole means 'this is not a time for haste and confusion.'
segments.s040-g207: ولیکن چنین گوید آن سالخورد — ولـیکن 'but/yet' introduces contrast. چنین 'thus/so' qualifies the reported speech. گوید here is the archaic/present-tense verb 'he says/said' (third person singular) used in narrative present; the subject آن سالخورد 'that aged one / the old man' follows (poetic inversion).
segments.s040-g208: که بودش سه فرزند آزاد مرد — که introduces a content clause 'that / who'; بودش is classical contraction meaning 'he had' or 'there was to him' (بود + enclitic -ش referring back to the old man). سه فرزند آزاد مرد = 'three sons, free/noble men' (numeral + noun phrase). The noun phrase uses آزاد as an epithet 'free/noble' modifying مرد.
segments.s040-g161: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('since/because'). از + noun is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of'; گوهر بد = 'a bad gem' (گوهر = gem, بد = bad). نیاید is a negative present/optative third‑person form meaning 'does not/should not come', and مهی is the predicate 'a moonlike one' (poetic metaphor for a beautiful person). The clause means 'because/from a bad gem a moon does not arise.'
segments.s040-g162: مرا + را here mark the recipient/emphasis (the input tokenization splits مرا into م + را; grammatically this is 'to me' / object-marking). دل is the theme 'heart'. همی is a small emphatic/aspectual particle (here adding immediacy/emphasis: 'indeed/just'); داد is the past verb 'gave'. این آگهی is the subject/agent phrase 'this news/information'. Literal order is marked/poetic, yielding 'this news gave (to) my heart' → natural 'this news gladdened my heart.'
segments.s040-g163: بگوی is an imperative/hortative form meaning 'say/tell!' (classical poetic imperative form). آن دو is 'those two'. ناپاک and بیهوده are stacked adjectives ('unclean/immoral' and 'worthless/useless'). را is the object marker attaching to the phrase: 'Tell those two unclean, worthless ones.'
segments.s040-g164: دو + اهریمن is the nominal description 'two devils'. مغز پالوده is a noun + adjective group literally 'with/whose brains are purified/refined' (here a descriptive epithet: 'with refined minds'). The trailing را repeats the object marking: these two noun phrases are in apposition, both objects of بگوی.
segments.s040-g165: انوشه is a vocative/proper name (addressing Anousheh). که introduces the subordinate clause (here relative/causal: 'who/since'). کردید is second‑person plural past 'you made/caused'. گوهر پدید is the object plus verbal complement 'the jewel to appear' (پدید = 'to appear/manifest'). Together: 'Anousheh, since you caused the jewel to appear...' (or 'Anousheh, you who brought forth the gem').
segments.s040-g166: درود 'greeting/praise' is the theme; از شما = 'from you' (source), and خود is emphatic 'yourself'/'indeed'. بدین‌سان (here segmented into ب + دین + سان) is the idiom 'in this manner/thus'. سزید is a verb meaning 'is deserved' / 'became due'. The clause says roughly 'such praise from you yourself is thus deserved.'
segments.s040-g167: ز پند من = 'from/my advice' (ز = poetic 'from/of', پند = 'advice', من = 'my'). ار is the conditional 'if'. مغزتان is 'your minds/brains' (possessed), شد = 'became'. تهی = 'empty/devoid'. The clause is conditional: 'If by my advice your minds became empty...' (i.e. if my counsel left your minds empty).
segments.s040-g168: همی is an emphatic/aspectual particle (here 'indeed' / marking consequence). از خردتان = 'of/ in your wisdom' (خردتان = your understanding). نبود is the negative past copula 'there was not'. آگهی here means 'awareness/knowledge/information'. The clause: 'then there was indeed no awareness in your wisdom.'
segments.s040-g145: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; هر کس که forms a relative clause 'whomever/anyone who'; پرسی is a present (2nd-person) verb 'you ask' (classical word order often keeps the verb later); به کام is an idiom 'to (one's) desire' and توانـد is 3rd-person singular 'is/shall be able' placed at the end as the finite verb, so the clause literally reads 'From whomever you ask, (he) can be to (your) desire.'
segments.s040-g146: همه 'everyone/all' begins the clause; پاک زنده is a stacked adjective phrase 'pure and alive'; به نام is an adverbial/idiomatic prepositional phrase 'by name / in name' (به here meaning 'by' or 'by means of'); توانـد again is the final finite verb 'can/able to', so this clause says roughly 'all, pure and alive, can be (so) by name' — i.e. 'can be named/are known.'
segments.s040-g147: منم is emphatic 'I am' (the pronoun and copula together); بنده‌ای is the indefinite self-reference 'a servant' (the -ی giving 'a/some'); شاه را + ناسزا is an elliptical noun phrase where را marks the king as the target and ناسزا is 'an insult' or 'insult directed at' — together 'an insult to the king' (the verb is omitted and picked up in the next segment).
segments.s040-g148: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following prepositional phrase; بر تن خویش is 'upon (my) own body' — note the possessive enclitic ش attaches to خوی (classical split orthography here) to give 'his/her/own'; ناپارسا is a predicate adjective 'impious/unholy'. Word order is emphatic/poetic: the prepositional phrase comes before the adjective, and the verb is again omitted (understood from context).
segments.s040-g149: پیامی is an indefinite direct object 'a message'; درشت here means 'coarse/rude' describing the message; آوریده is a literary past participle/compound meaning 'brought' (classical order places the participle after the object); به شاه marks the recipient 'to the king' — together 'a rude message was brought to the king.'
segments.s040-g150: فرستنده 'the sender/messenger' is the subject; پر + noun is a poetic adjectival construction meaning 'full (of)', so پر خشم = 'full of anger'; و من بیگناه is a coordinated clause with an omitted copula 'and I (am) innocent' — the verb 'am' is understood and not repeated.
segments.s040-g151: بگویم here functions as a performative 'I tell / I will say' (classical usage; the provided sense list did not match this use); چو is the subordinating particle 'as/when'; فرمایدم is a past form 'I said / I obeyed (the command)' — together چو فرمایدم شهریار reads 'as the king commanded (or as I told the king),' i.e. 'in accordance with the king's order.' Note classical word order and the ellipsis of some helpers common in poetry.
segments.s040-g152: پیام is 'message' and جوانان is the plural 'young men/young ones' (here 'young men'); ناهوشیار is an adjective 'unaware / heedless / foolish' modifying جوانان. The phrase is a simple noun phrase 'the message of the heedless youths' and completes the previous clause as what was reported.
segments.s040-g169: ندارید is the present-negative verb 'you do not have' (2nd person plural ending -ید) placed before its complements because of poetic inversion. "شرم و نه بیم" = "shame and not fear" functions as a coordinated object, and "از خدای" uses the preposition از with the genitive meaning 'of' to mark the source/object of the fear. Altogether: 'You do not have shame nor fear of God.'
segments.s040-g170: شما را is a topicalized/affected phrase marked by را (here functioning as emphasis/definite marker referring to 'you'), placed before the predicate. همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily', and همین‌ست is the copular phrase 'this is exactly'. رای is the noun 'counsel/plan' that is predicated by همین‌ست. Together: 'Verily, this is your counsel.'
segments.s040-g171: This line shows poetic inversion: مَرا (me/my) + پیشتر (formerly) precede the predicate قیرگون بود موی, where موی ('hair') is the grammatical subject appearing at the end. بود is the past copula 'was.' Thus the clause reads 'My hair was formerly pitch-black,' with مرا marking the possessor ('my').
segments.s040-g172: چو ... و چون introduce two similes: 'as/like a cypress in stature (چو سرو سهی قد)' and 'like the moon in face (چون ماه روی)'. سهی is a poetic epithet modifying قد (stature), and the second چون + ماه + روی gives the comparison for the face. Word order is poetic but the grammar is standard comparative.
segments.s040-g173: که introduces a relative clause modifying سپهری ('the Sepahri' or 'the warrior/celestial one'): 'سپهری که پشت مرا کرد' = 'the Sepahri who turned his back on me.' پشت + کردن is the idiom 'to turn one's back (on someone)'; here کرد is the past verb 'did/caused.' کوز follows as a noun in the clause (literal 'jar' — here likely a poetic image).
segments.s040-g174: نشد is the negative past/preterite of شدن ('did not become'); it negates the following predicate. The line links two coordinated results with و ('and'): '(he) did not become low (پست) and (his) گردان (troop) بجایست (was appropriate/fitting) نوز (newness/newborn).' بجایست is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'appropriate' or 'in its proper place.' The syntax is compressed and poetic but follows Persian coordination.
segments.s040-g175: خماند is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'remain' (or 'be left'); here it governs شما را (you, marked by را as the affected/beneficiary), with هم 'also' and این روزگار 'this age/time' as the topical time phrase. Word order is topicalized: 'May this age remain for you too' (or 'this age remained for you also').
segments.s040-g176: نماند is the negative present/future sense 'will not remain/last' (here used to say something will not endure). برین گونه = بر این گونه (in this manner), where the element ین is an orthographic variant of این attached to بر. بس is an intensifier ('very/enough') and پایدار is 'lasting/steadfast.' Together: 'Such steadfastness will not remain in this way.'
